stories.headline: Polis Declares Statewide Drought Emergency as Colorado Enters Phase 3
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis declared a statewide drought emergency Thursday, activating Phase 3 of Colorado's drought response plan amid what officials describe as one of the most severe droughts in the state's recorded history. Nearly 93% of Colorado is experiencing moderate to exceptional drought conditions, according to the June 4 U.S. Drought Monitor, with record-low snowpack, early snowmelt, extremely low river flows and critically dry soil moisture reported across all 64 counties.

The Phase 3 activation allows the state to deploy emergency funding for unmet needs, request a federal disaster declaration if necessary, and increases the frequency with which state agencies monitor conditions. Water restrictions are already in effect across most of the Denver metro area, and officials warned conditions are expected to worsen through the summer months.

Polis urged all Coloradans to use water wisely and said state agencies will reduce water usage at state facilities. The current water year, running from October 2025 to the present, has been the warmest on record in Colorado, contributing to elevated wildfire risk alongside the drought emergency.

stories.headline: Colorado Court of Appeals Orders Retrial for Paramedics Convicted in Elijah McClain's Death
stories.summary:
Two former Aurora Fire Rescue paramedics convicted in connection with the 2019 death of Elijah McClain will face new trials after the Colorado Court of Appeals reversed their criminally negligent homicide convictions. The appeals court found that the trial judge erred in failing to adequately define the standard of care after jurors indicated they did not understand what standard to apply, a ruling that tossed the homicide convictions for both Jeremy Cooper and Peter Cichuniec.

Cichuniec's second-degree assault conviction was affirmed by the appeals court and remains intact unless the state Supreme Court intervenes. He had originally been sentenced to five years in prison, later converted to four years of probation; Cooper received 14 months of work release and probation. Both are currently serving probationary sentences.

McClain, a 23-year-old Black man, died after Aurora police placed him in a carotid hold during a 2019 street contact and paramedics injected him with ketamine. His family's advocate questioned why a retrial is warranted when neither man is incarcerated. Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser said a jury had already held the paramedics accountable for the death of 'an innocent Black man who did nothing wrong that tragic night seven years ago.'

stories.headline: Head of Colorado's Office of the Alternate Defense Counsel Placed on Paid Leave Amid Investigation
stories.summary:
The commission overseeing Colorado's Office of the Alternate Defense Counsel voted Thursday to place executive director Joanna Landau on paid administrative leave while an investigation into her leadership is conducted. The action follows a May 22 public meeting in which attorneys from Colorado's indigent defense community spent two hours publicly criticizing Landau for a culture shift they say threatens the quality of indigent criminal defense in the state.

The nine-member volunteer commission announced a formal investigation last week and moved quickly to suspend Landau from her duties, citing concern that her continued presence would impede the agency's core mission. Commission member John Ponikvar said the situation had made it difficult for Landau to positively impact the agency and that clients must continue to be protected. Deputy Director Darren Cantor was named acting executive director.

Landau said she supports the investigation and will not challenge the decision, framing the public backlash as expected resistance to change after she succeeded a leader who had held the role for two decades. The commission set a Monday deadline for investigative firms to submit proposals, with the probe to examine Landau's performance, workplace environment, specific complaints, potential employment discrimination and inaccurate public statements.

stories.headline: Jefferson County Man Sentenced to Six Years for Pimping in Denver-Area Sex Trafficking Case
stories.summary:
Cortez 'Tez' D'Angelo Dennis, 32, was sentenced to six years in the Colorado Department of Corrections after pleading guilty in February to pimping, a felony, following an undercover operation by the Jefferson County Sheriff's Office. A second felony charge of human trafficking for sexual servitude was dropped as part of his plea agreement.

The investigation began in January 2025 when an undercover officer arranged a meeting through the commercial sex website Secret Hostess and two women arrived at a south Jefferson County house, where they were met by sheriff's officials and victim advocates. Both women identified Dennis as their pimp; one told investigators she had been recruited into prostitution at age 14.

Detectives traced Dennis' operations to a residence in Centennial linked to his Venmo account. He was formally arrested in September 2025 after a months-long investigation. The case highlights ongoing law enforcement efforts targeting commercial sex exploitation in the Denver metro region.

stories.headline: Denver Developer Breaks Ground on $400M Mixed-Use Project Near RiNo Light Rail Stop
stories.summary:
Denver-based Formativ CEO Sean Campbell broke ground Wednesday on a 16-story, 358-unit market-rate apartment tower at 38th and Blake streets in the RiNo neighborhood, the second of two apartment buildings rising simultaneously on a 2.1-acre site adjacent to RTD's 38th and Blake rail station. The broader development is valued at approximately $400 million and also includes a previously started income-restricted apartment building and a 180-room hotel slated to begin construction early next year.

The market-rate tower, which will cost $196 million to develop, will feature fifth-floor and rooftop decks, a sauna and cold plunge. The income-restricted building targets residents earning 60% to 90% of area median income and received no public funding — an unusual financing structure Campbell attributed to strong private partners. The site will also include 15,000 square feet of ground-floor retail and three restaurants, along with a pedestrian promenade connecting directly to the rail stop.

Campbell began assembling the site in 2016 and was preparing to launch the project when the COVID-19 pandemic halted construction. The World Trade Center Denver, originally slated to anchor the campus, announced in 2021 it would locate elsewhere. RiNo has seen a handful of other apartment groundbreakings recently, including a 13-story, 301-unit building started two blocks away in April by developer Jon Dwight.

stories.headline: King Soopers Opens $37M Virginia Village Store as Belcaro Site Eyes Apartment Redevelopment
stories.summary:
King Soopers debuted a new $37 million store at 4201 E. Arkansas Ave. in Denver's Virginia Village neighborhood Wednesday, one day after closing its longtime Belcaro location at 825 S. Colorado Blvd., which had operated for more than 50 years. The new store was developed by Denver-based Kentro Group on the former Colorado Department of Transportation headquarters site, where construction began in early 2025.

Kentro is poised to acquire the 7-acre Belcaro site that King Soopers has vacated. Plans submitted to Denver show San Antonio-based Embrey seeking to build a 4-story, 347-unit apartment complex on five of those acres, replacing the strip mall where the grocery store operated. Kentro agreed not to rezone the site following pushback from neighbors.

A separate 1.3-acre parcel adjacent to the Belcaro site is being eyed by shoe retailer Dardano's, which is considering demolishing an existing vacant liquor store building and constructing a 15,000-square-foot replacement. Kentro co-founder Jimmy Balafas said development deals for the site are progressing but that uncertainty remains.

stories.headline: Denver Realtors: Interest Rates, Not Prices, Are the Primary Obstacle in Metro Housing Market
stories.summary:
The Denver Metro Association of Realtors' May Market Trends report shows the median price of a single-family home in the 11-county metro area rose to $675,000, up from $665,000 in April, while closed sales fell 4.32% month-over-month and sit nearly 7% below year-ago levels. DMAR Market Trends Committee chair Amanda Snitker said prices are tracking the market's long-run historical appreciation trajectory and that stubbornly high mortgage rates — not prices — are the central affordability problem.

Snitker noted that homeowners locked into 3%-4% mortgages face monthly payment increases of $1,500 to $2,000 on a typical move-up purchase, discouraging them from listing. New listings fell nearly 18% year-over-year, though total inventory still reached 12,259 available homes, up 6% over the month. The median-priced listing continued to go under contract in 14 days.

DMAR advised buyers to focus on rate solutions rather than waiting for a significant price correction, noting that tools such as seller concessions, inspection contingencies and rate buydown negotiations are again available. At the high end of the market, luxury properties priced above $1 million showed relative strength, with pending and closed transactions both up compared to the prior period.

stories.headline: Former Bubba Gump's Building in Downtown Denver Sells for First Time Since 1962
stories.summary:
The former Bubba Gump Shrimp Co. building in downtown Denver has sold for $1.8 million to an LLC controlled by Amnon Ben-Ari, marking the property's first change of ownership since 1962. The buyer is planning a new restaurant concept for the space, though details of the concept have not been disclosed.

The sale represents a potential new chapter for a downtown restaurant location that has struggled in recent years amid broader challenges facing Denver's central business district. The building's decades-long single-ownership tenure and its prime downtown position made the transaction notable in local real estate circles.

The sale adds to a mix of signals in downtown Denver's commercial real estate market, where vacancy rates remain elevated but individual properties continue to attract investment from local operators seeking to reposition assets.

stories.headline: Avalanche Sign Russian Goaltender Nikita Novosyolov to Two-Year Entry-Level Contract
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche have signed goaltender Nikita Novosyolov to a two-year entry-level contract, the team announced. Novosyolov is a Russian netminder who will enter the organization as the Avalanche continue to build organizational depth at the position.

The signing comes as the Avalanche front office undergoes transition following the departure of longtime general manager Chris MacFarland to Nashville, with Joe Sakic having resumed GM duties. Adding a young goaltending prospect is part of the team's effort to replenish the organizational pipeline.

Entry-level contract signings of this nature typically place the player in the minor league system initially, with advancement contingent on performance and organizational need. Specific contract terms, including salary details, were not disclosed.

stories.headline: Ballpark General Improvement District Marks First Anniversary, Opens Neighborhood Office
stories.summary:
Denver's Ballpark General Improvement District held a ribbon-cutting ceremony Thursday to open a new neighborhood office, coinciding with the release of its inaugural annual report after one year of operations. The district, funded by a tax collected from businesses and residents in the roughly 40-block neighborhood surrounding Coors Field, supports more than 130 independently owned local businesses.

In its first year, the district's ambassador team collected more than 22,000 pounds of litter, removed 7,000 graffiti tags and posters, serviced 500 trash cans and surveyed and watered more than 1,000 trees. The district also invested in additional street lighting and safety escort services for the neighborhood.

Executive Director Kate McKenna said the new community office will serve as a base of operations for staff and ambassadors while also being made available to other community organizations. Operations Manager Luke McCarthy said the improvement in neighborhood conditions since ambassadors first deployed on March 10, 2025 has been dramatic, describing the change as 'almost night and day.'

stories.headline: Northern Lights May Be Visible in Colorado Tonight as G3 Solar Storm Approaches
stories.summary:
Space weather forecasters say a trio of coronal mass ejections are expected to produce aurora borealis activity visible in Colorado on Friday night, though the storm is projected to be less intense than the widely photographed November display. The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's Space Weather Prediction Center estimates the solar activity will peak at approximately 8 p.m. before slowly diminishing through the night.

The incoming storms are rated at G3, classified as 'strong,' which typically allows aurora to be seen as far south as Wyoming and Nebraska. November's event reached G4, or 'severe,' and lit Colorado skies with vivid pink, purple, blue and green displays visible to the naked eye statewide.

FOAA forecasters recommended getting away from city lights and finding dark locations for the best viewing chances. They also noted that smartphone cameras can sometimes capture aurora that is too faint to see with the naked eye, potentially extending the viewing opportunity even during a less intense event.

stories.headline: Denver Pride Month Kicks Off with Events Across Metro; 60th Annual Greek Festival Opens This Weekend
stories.summary:
Denver Pride Month is underway with a monthlong calendar of events replacing the traditional single-weekend festival format, including a Pride Prom at the Denver Zoo, an art crawl, a queer hike, a pickleball tournament and the Mutt Strutt doggy drag show. Events are distributed across June, building toward a major end-of-month celebration, with Pride activities also taking place in Fort Collins, Longmont, Leadville, Carbondale, Aspen and Glenwood Springs this weekend.

Also opening this weekend is the 60th annual Denver Greek Festival at Assumption of the Theotokos Greek Orthodox Cathedral, 4610 E. Alameda Ave., running Friday through Sunday. The event, which drew record attendance of more than 30,000 last year, features an expanded footprint, indoor dining, traditional dance and music, cathedral tours, cooking demonstrations and new food offerings. Admission is $3 to $5 per day; children 12 and under are free.

Additionally, Meow Wolf Denver launches its summer Phenomenomaly programming Friday, running Wednesdays through Sundays through Aug. 9, with roving performers, dancers, puppeteers and artists active inside the Convergence Station exhibition from 2 to 7 p.m. on selected dates. The inaugural performances June 5-7 feature Love Art City, an Afro-futurist movement house.

weather.notable: Nearly 8 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: My family doesn't really do hugs. We just kind of look at each other and go, 'Alright, well, I'll see you later.'

weekendEvents.title: Denver Chalk Art Festival
weekendEvents.description: More than 200 professional and amateur artists transform the streets of the Golden Triangle Creative District into a colorful street museum using vivid pastel chalks. The free festival covers two blocks south of Civic Center Park near 12th and Bannock.
weekendEvents.location: Golden Triangle Creative District (12th & Bannock), Denver

weekendEvents.title: Brit Floyd at Red Rocks Amphitheatre
weekendEvents.description: Brit Floyd delivers one of the most celebrated Pink Floyd tribute experiences in the world, recreating the music and visual spectacle of Pink Floyd with full production detail.

weekendEvents.title: Big Head Todd & The Monsters with 4 Non Blondes at Red Rocks
weekendEvents.description: Colorado rock institution Big Head Todd & The Monsters headlines Red Rocks in a hometown celebration, joined by 4 Non Blondes. The band has performed at Red Rocks more than 30 times since 1991.

weekendEvents.title: Mumford & Sons at Red Rocks Amphitheatre
weekendEvents.description: Global folk-rock act Mumford & Sons performs at Red Rocks with support from Sierra Ferrell and Dylan Gossett, featuring their catalog of anthemic, sing-along material.

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Rockies Pride Night vs. Milwaukee Brewers
weekendEvents.description: The Colorado Rockies host the Milwaukee Brewers in a three-game series at Coors Field, with Friday night designated as Pride Night at the ballpark.

weekendEvents.title: Tchami at The Church Nightclub
weekendEvents.description: French electronic producer Tchami, a pioneer of future house who has collaborated with DJ Snake and Martin Garrix, takes over The Church Nightclub for a late-night dance floor set.

weekendEvents.title: Summer Sessions at Riverfront Park
weekendEvents.description: A free outdoor festival in downtown Denver's Riverfront Park featuring live music, cold drinks, and an open-air crowd to kick off the summer season.

weekendEvents.title: Greeley Blues Jam Music Festival
weekendEvents.description: A celebration of blues music in Northern Colorado featuring performers from Colorado and around the country, with music, food, and vendors. Pre-festival activities begin at 4:30 p.m. Friday on the 9th Street Plaza in Historic Downtown Greeley.

weekendEvents.title: Bob Schneider at Bluebird Theatre
weekendEvents.description: Veteran singer-songwriter Bob Schneider performs at the Bluebird Theatre in a show open to attendees 16 and over.

weekendEvents.title: Edgewater Pride
weekendEvents.description: Pride festivities in Edgewater begin with a parade at 10:30 a.m., followed by live music, drag performances, pop-up booths, and a Pride Party through the afternoon.

weekendEvents.title: Boulder Jewish Festival
weekendEvents.description: The longest-running cultural festival in Boulder celebrates Jewish heritage with music, artists, food, and community on Pearl Street Mall.

weekendEvents.title: Park Hill Art Festival
weekendEvents.description: An outdoor art festival in Denver's Park Hill neighborhood featuring works from local and regional artists across a range of mediums.

topStory.headline: House Passes Ukraine Aid Bill in Second Major Foreign Policy Break With Trump This Week
topStory.summary:
The U.S. House of Representatives passed legislation Thursday to provide aid to Ukraine and impose sanctions on key segments of the Russian economy, defying Republican leadership in a 226-195 vote. Eighteen Republicans crossed party lines to join Democrats in backing the Ukraine Support Act, sponsored by Rep. Gregory Meeks, D-N.Y. The bill would deliver more than $1 billion in security and reconstruction aid and authorize $8 billion in financing loans for Ukraine's defense operations.

Supporters forced the vote through a discharge petition, a legislative tool that allows a majority of the House to bypass leadership when 218 signatures are obtained. The maneuver has been used several times this Congress to advance bills on topics including the release of Jeffrey Epstein files and extension of Affordable Care Act health care subsidies. Republican leaders opposed the measure, with House Foreign Affairs Committee Chairman Brian Mast, R-Fla., calling it "a cudgel to fight against President Trump" and an "unserious bill" drafted roughly a year and a half ago. Rep. French Hill, R-Ark., argued the legislation was flawed and outdated and could reduce NATO spending.

Supporters of the bill made the case in moral and strategic terms. Rep. Don Bacon, R-Neb., who broke with his party to vote yes, framed the choice as one between good and evil. Rep. Gregory Meeks argued the core question was whether the House would help Ukraine negotiate from a position of strength or allow Russia to outlast American resolve. Rep. Brian Fitzpatrick, who signed the discharge petition, said the vote would send a message to Ukrainian soldiers and signal to Russian President Vladimir Putin that support for Ukraine remains alive in Congress.

The vote marks the second significant foreign policy rebuke of President Trump by the House in as many days. On Wednesday, the House approved a war powers resolution seeking to halt U.S. military action against Iran, the first time such a resolution has passed the chamber. The White House dismissed that resolution as an unconstitutional restriction on presidential power.

Despite its passage in the House, the Ukraine Support Act faces long odds of becoming law. The bill must clear the Senate, where it is unlikely to reach the 60 votes needed to advance without presidential backing, and would then require Trump's signature. Supporters nonetheless expressed hope that the House vote would pressure the Senate to take up the issue. The legislation comes as ceasefire negotiations between Russia and Ukraine remain stalled, with talks in Geneva, Abu Dhabi, and Istanbul having failed and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky calling Thursday for a direct face-to-face meeting with Vladimir Putin.
